content.problemStatement:
Given an array of integers, sort it in ascending order using the Bubble Sort algorithm.


Input Format
The first line contains an integer n, denoting the size of the array.

The second line contains n space-separated integers, denoting the elements of the array.



Output Format
Print the sorted array in ascending order, with space-separated integers.
content.inputFormat: The first line contains an integer T (1 ≤ T ≤ 10), the number of test cases. For each test case, the first line contains an integer n (1 ≤ n ≤ 1000), the size of the array. The second line contains n space‑separated integers (1 ≤ arr[i] ≤ 1000).
content.outputFormat: For each test case, output a single line with the n integers of the sorted array in ascending order, separated by a single space.

sampleTestCases.explanation:
Three test cases are processed.
- Test case 1: after bubble sort the array becomes [2,3,4,5,8].
- Test case 2: the array is already sorted.
- Test case 3: sorting [4,3,2,1] yields [1,2,3,4].
A single element array is trivially sorted.
First test case demonstrates sorting a mixed array. Second test case shows that an array with all equal elements remains unchanged.

solution.approach: For each test case we apply the classic Bubble Sort algorithm. We repeatedly scan the array, swapping adjacent elements that are out of order. After the i‑th pass the largest i elements are already at the end, so the inner loop can stop at n‑i‑1. The process continues for n‑1 passes, guaranteeing a fully sorted array. The algorithm is simple to implement and matches the problem statement which explicitly asks for Bubble Sort.
solution.timeComplexity: O(T * n^2) – each test case performs at most (n‑1) passes with up to (n‑i‑1) comparisons per pass.
solution.spaceComplexity: O(1) – sorting is done in‑place, only a few auxiliary variables are used.
